original_article:
TOKYO (Reuters) — Tens of thousands of workers at Japan's leading automotive manufacturers walked off production lines on Monday in what union leaders are calling the largest coordinated labor action in the country's automotive sector since 1975.

Toyota Industries Corp confirmed that production at its Hekinan and Obu plants in Aichi Prefecture has been suspended indefinitely after approximately 8,000 employees joined the nationwide strike. The company produces critical powertrain components, forklifts, and textile machinery at these facilities.

"Negotiations with management have reached an impasse over wage restructuring and the transition to electric vehicle production lines," said Akira Koike, chairman of the Japanese Automobile Workers' Union (JAW). "Our members have no choice but to exercise their right to industrial action."

Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) estimated that the strikes have affected production at over 30 automotive manufacturing facilities across the country, with daily output losses exceeding ¥45 billion ($300 million). Parts suppliers across Japan's automotive ecosystem are reporting cascading disruptions as just-in-time inventory systems run dry.

The Japan Automobile Manufacturers Association (JAMA) called an emergency meeting for Thursday to assess the impact on global vehicle delivery schedules.
paraphrases:
TOKYO — Japan's automotive industry is facing its worst labor disruption in nearly five decades after a coordinated strike shut down production lines at major manufacturers. Toyota Industries Corp confirmed indefinite production halts at its Hekinan and Obu plants in Aichi Prefecture, with approximately 8,000 workers walking off the job.

The Japanese Automobile Workers' Union said negotiations over wages and the transition to electric vehicle manufacturing have broken down completely. Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry estimated daily output losses exceeding ¥45 billion across more than 30 affected facilities.

Parts suppliers operating under just-in-time inventory systems are reporting cascading shortages as stockpiles run out. The Japan Automobile Manufacturers Association has convened an emergency session to evaluate the impact on international delivery commitments.

TOKYO (AP) — A massive labor strike across Japan's automotive sector has halted production at more than 30 manufacturing facilities, in what union leaders describe as the largest coordinated industrial action since 1975.

Toyota Industries Corp said its Hekinan and Obu plants in Aichi Prefecture are suspended indefinitely after 8,000 employees joined the walkout. The company produces powertrain components, forklifts, and industrial equipment at these sites.

Akira Koike, chairman of the Japanese Automobile Workers' Union, said the dispute centres on wage restructuring and job security amid the shift toward electric vehicle production. Japan's trade ministry has estimated that the strikes are costing the automotive sector approximately ¥45 billion per day, with just-in-time supply chains running dry across the industry.

Production across Japan's automotive manufacturing ecosystem has ground to a halt as tens of thousands of workers stage a coordinated strike that industry observers are calling unprecedented in modern Japanese labour relations.

Toyota Industries Corp—a major producer of powertrain systems, compressors, and material handling equipment—confirmed that operations at its Hekinan and Obu plants are suspended indefinitely. The strike has spread to over 30 automotive facilities nationwide.

The Japanese Automobile Workers' Union cited failed negotiations over compensation restructuring and concerns about workforce displacement from EV production transitions. METI officials estimate daily losses at ¥45 billion ($300 million). The Japan Automobile Manufacturers Association has called an emergency meeting to assess delivery disruptions.
